summary: The bile duct is a crucial anatomic landmark used by Couinaud’s system to differentiate between liver segments due to unique branching patterns associated with individual segments; however, both arterial supply via the hepatic arteries as well as venous drainage through various structures also contribute but are less definitive than biliary tree variations alone do when identifying distinct sections within this complex organ division scheme proposed originally based upon detailed surgical observations made during transplantation procedures decades ago now widely adopted clinically worldwide today despite some ongoing debate over exact boundaries sometimes seen across literature reviews comparing findings among studies using CT scans versus direct visualization techniques performed at surgery itself which may account somewhat differently depending who exactly defines them precisely given inherent variability noted amongst individuals studied thus far leading researchers continue refining these classifications further still while maintaining core principles established initially underpinned fundamentally around understanding functional units formed naturally throughout development process resulting ultimately in current accepted standards utilized routinely nowadays especially important contextually speaking particularly concerning hepatobiliary diseases requiring precise localization knowledge necessary preoperatively planning interventions accurately targeting affected areas without risking damage elsewhere potentially life-threatening consequences otherwise avoided altogether thanks largely efforts pioneering work done long time past continuing legacy lives on shaping modern practice guiding future advancements alike all stemming comprising adult mammalian liver efficiently manageable chunks allowing surgeons operate safely effectively treating pathology affecting any particular region reliably predict outcomes
